cells.headline: Genus-2 twister complex_volume re-run succeeds and numerically confirms the banked orientation-reversal structure (vol equal, CS opposite, CS!=0 matching confirmed chirality); the original None/failure was a usage artifact (CS-initialization ordering for closed manifolds), not a non-existence of the invariant.
cells.discriminating_fact: For g2 = snappy.twister.Surface("S_2").bundle("a*B*c*d*E") (closed via cusp filling (1,0)), calling g2.complex_volume() directly reproduces the original failure verbatim -- ValueError("The Chern-Simons invariant isn't currently known.") -- EVEN when solution_type is "all tetrahedra positively oriented", proving the failure was never about geometric structure. SnapPy's own chern_simons() docstring names the exact mechanism: for a closed manifold, chern_simons()/complex_volume() require SnapPea's internal CS "fudge factor" to be initialized on the UNFILLED cusped triangulation before the closing Dehn filling is (re)applied. Applying that fix (copy -> dehn_fill((0,0)) -> chern_simons() -> dehn_fill back to (1,0) -> complex_volume()) on TWO independent triangulations (the native twister triangulation and an independently M.randomize()'d retriangulation, 11 vs 11/10 tetrahedra) gives complex_volume = 7.5176898965 - 2.3816866587*I both times, agreeing to |diff| ~ 3e-14 (tol used: 1e-9). The orientation-reversed manifold (reverse_orientation()), same two-seed protocol, gives 7.5176898965 + 2.3816866587*I (mirror-seed agreement ~1.6e-15).
